items.q: Is a catalyst used up during the reaction it speeds up?
items.correct: No — it is chemically unchanged at the end and can be reused; it works by providing a lower-energy pathway.
items.misconception: Catalysts are consumed like reactants, so more must keep being added.
items.random_wrong: Catalysts gradually turn into the product during the reaction.
items.why: Students place the catalyst alongside reactants in their mental equation; it participates temporarily but is regenerated.

items.q: Does increasing concentration make the particles move faster?
items.correct: No — it packs more particles into the same volume so collisions are more frequent; only temperature changes particle speed and energy.
items.misconception: A higher concentration speeds the reaction because the particles move faster.
items.random_wrong: A higher concentration lowers the activation energy of the reaction.
items.why: Students merge all rate factors into 'particles get faster'; each factor works through a distinct collision-theory mechanism.

items.q: Is energy released when the bonds in a fuel break?
items.correct: No — breaking bonds absorbs energy; the heat from burning comes from the stronger new bonds formed in the products releasing more energy than bond breaking absorbs.
items.misconception: Burning releases the energy stored inside the fuel's bonds as they break.
items.random_wrong: Both bond breaking and bond making release energy.
items.why: The phrase 'energy stored in bonds' misleads; exothermic reactions are exothermic because bond making outweighs bond breaking.

items.q: The temperature of a reaction mixture rises as the reaction proceeds. The reaction is:
items.why: Correct answer: Exothermic. “Endothermic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these does NOT increase the rate of a reaction?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Decreasing the temperature. “Increasing the concentration” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A catalyst speeds up a reaction by:
items.correct: Providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy
items.why: It's Providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. “Heating the reaction mixture” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In a reversible reaction at dynamic equilibrium:
items.correct: The forward and backward rates are equal
items.random_wrong: Reactant and product concentrations must be equal
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The forward and backward rates are equal. “Both reactions have stopped” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A reaction warms 100 g of water by 5.0 °C (c = 4.2 J g⁻¹ °C⁻¹). Calculate the heat released, q = mcΔT, in joules.
items.why: The real figure is 2100 J. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
